text:
Epilepsy Centre

Neurosciences Division


From: Dr Priya Shah (KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST)
Sent: 20 November 2017 10:15
To: kch.neuro-epilepsy@nhs.net
Cc: Dr Michael Grant (KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST); Dr Elena Rossi (KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST)
Subject: Review following supply issues and ongoing seizure burden

Hi Priya

Epilepsy Diagnosis:

Combined generalised and focal epilepsy – the patient has both generalised and focal epilepsy, with history of focal aware sensory episodes evolving at times, alongside generalised tonic-clonic seizures, morning myoclonic clusters, and epileptic spasms.

Present Medication:

1. Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily (modified-release)
2. Topiramate 100 mg at night
3. Clobazam 10 mg at night as needed for clusters (used 1–2 days per week on average)

Present Seizure Frequency:

Her seizure control is variable, she continues to experience epileptic spasm on one or two per day, occasional generalised tonic-clonic seizures, and myoclonic jerks in morning clusters, this pattern has remained unchanged for several years. She and her family report that day-to-day variability is often worse when there are delays or substitutions in pharmacy supply, and historical adherence was impacted at times due to difficulties obtaining consistent formulations.

Plan of Action:

- Formulation consistency: She reports past variability in pharmacy supply (brand switches and short-dated stock) coincided with perceived worsening of myoclonic clusters and intermittent missed doses. To reduce variability, please prescribe lamotrigine as a specific brand (modified-release) and annotate ‘do not substitute’ on the prescription. We have provided her with a written medication schedule and a 7-day pill organiser to support adherence during any future supply disruptions.

- Monitoring and safety: She will maintain a seizure and trigger diary noting supply issues, missed doses, and any adverse effects. We have requested serum lamotrigine level in 2–3 weeks if there are further supply changes or clinical deterioration. Bone health and contraception were discussed; she remains on folic acid 5 mg daily.

- Rescue and clusters: Clobazam remains available for short-term use during clusters. She understands not to exceed recommended dosing and to seek help if clusters persist beyond her usual pattern or if there is any injury or prolonged post-ictal confusion.

- Follow-up: Telephone review in 8 weeks to assess stability with consistent supply and adherence measures, with face-to-face appointment in 4 months. If variability persists despite stable supply and adherence, we will consider rationalising therapy (e.g., cautious topiramate down-titration with introduction of an alternative adjunct) after review of seizure diary and drug levels.

Please let us know if there are continuing supply constraints so we can coordinate with pharmacy in advance.

With best wishes

entities.text: she continues to experience epileptic spasm on one or two per day

Her seizure control is variable, she continues to experience epileptic spasm on one or two per day, occasional generalised tonic-clonic seizures, and myoclonic jerks in morning clusters, this pattern has remained unchanged for several years

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter states: "she continues to experience epileptic spasm on one or two per day, occasional generalised tonic-clonic seizures, and myoclonic jerks in morning clusters." The only explicit numeric frequency is for epileptic spasms: "one or two per day." Other seizure types are described as occasional or in clusters without a quantifiable per-time frequency. Per the rule to select the highest frequency when multiple types exist, the daily epileptic spasms (1 to 2 per day) represent the highest measurable frequency. Therefore, the normalized label is "1 to 2 per day."
seizure_frequency_number: she continues to experience epileptic spasm on one or two per day
reference: Her seizure control is variable, she continues to experience epileptic spasm on one or two per day, occasional generalised tonic-clonic seizures, and myoclonic jerks in morning clusters, this pattern has remained unchanged for several years
